name: Montes Torozos Castles and Monasteries Loop
name_original: Pueblos olvidados de Valladolid – circular desde Castromonte
description: Explore the forgotten villages of Valladolid on this 35.7-mile (57.5 km) circular touring cycling route from Castromonte.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Castromonte traverse the varied landscapes of the Valladolid province in Castilla y León. The region features a mix of flat plains, rolling hills, and areas with oak and poplar groves, as well as expansive cereal fields. The River Bajoz flows nearby, offering potential for routes along its banks. Roads in this area often have low vehicle density, contributing to a more tranquil cycling experience. Best touring cycling routes around Castromonte The most popular touring cycling route is Montes Torozos Castles and Monasteries Loop, a 35.7 miles (57.5 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 21 minutes to complete. This route connects historical sites within the Montes Torozos region. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Canal de Castilla – circular desde Medina del Rioseco, an easy 13.4 miles (21.6 km) path. This route follows the historic Canal de Castilla, offering flat terrain and waterside views. Local touring cyclists also love the Monastery of La Santa Espina – Canal de Castilla Dock loop from Medina de Rioseco, a 32.9 miles (53.0 km) trail leading through agricultural landscapes and past historical points, often completed in about 4 hours 26 minutes. Touring cycling around Castromonte is defined by its mix of flat plains, gentle rolling hills, and routes along the Canal de Castilla. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more challenging loops through the Montes Torozos. The routes in Castromonte are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 40 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castromonte's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Castromonte?
A: There are 13 touring cycling routes around Castromonte, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Castromonte?
A: The terrain around Castromonte is quite varied, ranging from flat plains and gentle rolling hills to routes that follow the historic Canal de Castilla. You'll cycle through oak groves, poplar groves, pine forests, and expansive cereal fields, often on roads with low vehicle density for a more tranquil experience.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Castromonte offers several easy routes. A great option for beginners or families is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175166816/canal-de-castilla-circular-desde-medina-del-rioseco" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla – circular desde Medina del Rioseco&lt;/a&gt;. This 13.4-mile (21.6 km) path follows the historic Canal de Castilla, providing flat terrain and scenic waterside views, ideal for a relaxed ride.

Q: Can I find more challenging touring cycling routes in the Castromonte area?
A: For those seeking a more challenging ride, the region provides options with greater distances and elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24549731" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of La Santa Espina – Santa Espina Reservoir loop from Urueña&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 39-mile (62.7 km) route that includes more significant climbs and descents.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I explore along the cycling routes?
A: Cycling routes around Castromonte often pass by significant historical and natural attractions. You can visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3754394" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of La Santa Espina&lt;/a&gt;, explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/890605" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla Dock&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2981131" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bajoz River Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, routes in the Montes Torozos region may lead you past various castles, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5709148" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Urueña Castle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170803286/montes-torozos-castles-and-monasteries-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montes Torozos Castles and Monasteries Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 35.7-mile (57.5 km) moderate trail that connects historical sites in a circular fashion.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling around Castromonte?
A: The region of Castilla y León, where Castromonte is located, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are particularly beautiful, with blooming flora in spring and vibrant colors in autumn. Summers can be hot, and winters can be chilly, so plan accordingly.

Q: Are there any routes that focus on the Canal de Castilla?
A: Absolutely. The Canal de Castilla is a prominent feature in the region, offering excellent cycling experiences. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31877373" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of La Santa Espina – Canal de Castilla Dock loop from Medina de Rioseco&lt;/a&gt; is a 32.9-mile (53.0 km) route that incorporates sections along the canal, combining historical exploration with waterside cycling.

Q: What amenities are available for cyclists in Castromonte or nearby towns?
A: Castromonte is a small village, but its surroundings offer amenities in larger nearby towns like Medina de Rioseco. You can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options there. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes, as services might be sparse in more rural sections.

Q: Is it possible to combine cycling with visits to cultural sites?
A: Yes, the touring cycling routes around Castromonte are perfect for combining outdoor activity with cultural exploration. Many routes are designed to pass by historical villages, ancient monasteries, and charming churches, allowing you to immerse yourself in the rich heritage of Castilla y León while enjoying your ride. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38173189" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Following the Canal de Castille – Church of Santa Cruz loop from Medina de Rioseco&lt;/a&gt; offers a blend of natural beauty and historical stops.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a cycling tour in Castromonte?
A: As a small village, Castromonte typically offers street parking. For longer tours, it's often convenient to park in larger nearby towns like Medina de Rioseco, which serve as common starting points for many regional cycling routes and usually have more designated parking areas.

Q: What makes touring cycling in Castromonte unique?
A: Touring cycling in Castromonte is unique due to its blend of tranquil village charm, historical significance, and diverse landscapes. The low vehicle density on many roads contributes to a peaceful cycling experience, allowing you to fully appreciate the region's cultural heritage, from ancient Roman settlements to medieval monasteries, alongside the natural beauty of the River Bajoz and expansive plains.
tips: Built on the ruins of a Roman fort, hence the name Castroalmonde, the most notable monument is near the Plaza de la Constitución and admires the 16th-century Church of the Immaculate Conception.

The Castle of the Comuneros rises majestically above the Torozos Mountains, visible from several kilometers around. Located in Torrelobatón, in the province of Valladolid, this castle has become the symbol of the town and is one of the best preserved in Castilla y León. It was declared Historical Heritage in 1949.
This imposing castle, a witness to history and struggle, remains a fascinating attraction for visitors. If you ever have the opportunity, I recommend exploring its walls and discovering the secrets it keeps in its centuries-old stone. 🏰
The monastery owes its name to the relic that, extracted from the holy crown of Christ, has been housed in one of its chapels for almost nine centuries. Today it is still an object of veneration.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The 'Forgotten villages of Valladolid – circular from Castromonte' route is approximately 57.5 kilometers long and typically takes around 3 hours and 21 minutes to complete by touring bicycle. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your cycling speed, stops, and fitness level.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate, featuring varied terrain. While some sections are likely paved, you should expect unpaved segments, including gravel or dirt roads, which are common in rural and historical areas of Valladolid. It requires good fitness due to these varied surfaces and some elevation changes.

faqs.question: What historical landmarks and sights can I see on this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: The route is designed to explore 'forgotten villages' with centuries-old stone structures and traditional architecture. Notable highlights include the Church of the Immaculate Conception of Castromonte, the impressive Torrelobatón Castle, and the significant religious site of the Monastery of La Santa Espina. These offer a unique glimpse into the region's rich history and culture.

faqs.question: Where does the Forgotten villages of Valladolid route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular tour starting and ending in Castromonte. You can typically find parking within the village of Castromonte itself, near the main square or other public areas, to begin your cycling adventure. Specific trailhead parking information is not provided, but Castromonte serves as the primary access point.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'moderate' and requires good fitness due to its length (57.5 km), varied terrain including unpaved sections, and an elevation gain of approximately 307 meters. While not extremely challenging, it might be demanding for absolute beginners. Cyclists with some experience and a reasonable fitness level will enjoy it more.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Forgotten villages of Valladolid route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, the weather in the Valladolid province is typically milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter. This makes for a more enjoyable cycling experience through the rural landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route or in the Montes Torozos area?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and established rural routes in the Castilla y León region, including the Montes Torozos area where this trail is located. However, always respect private property and any local signage you encounter.

faqs.question: Is the Forgotten villages of Valladolid cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route primarily follows public roads and rural tracks, the suitability for dogs depends on their ability to keep up with a touring bicycle for 57.5 km and the specific regulations of any villages or private lands you pass through. If your dog is accustomed to long distances and you can ensure their safety (e.g., in a bike trailer), it might be possible, but it's not explicitly designed as a dog-friendly trail for dogs running alongside a bike.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other official paths. You'll find short sections that overlap with the Madrid Way of Saint James, Por los Páramos de Torozos, Senda del Embalse del Río Bajoz, and the Cañada Real Leonesa Occidental, among others. These intersections are typically minor portions of the overall route.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay along the route or in nearby villages?
faqs.answer: As the route passes through several 'forgotten villages,' you may find limited options for cafes, restaurants, or accommodation directly on the trail. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks. However, Castromonte, as the starting point, and other larger villages in the Valladolid province, will offer more services for refreshments and overnight stays.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: For this moderate touring bicycle route, it's recommended to bring a well-maintained bicycle suitable for varied terrain, a helmet, sufficient water (especially on warmer days), snacks, basic repair tools (spare tube, pump), sun protection, and appropriate clothing for the weather. A map or GPS device is also helpful for navigation, though the Komoot app can guide you.

waypoints.name: Church of the Immaculate Conception of Castromonte
